task.title: Setting Up Authoring Tools
task.shortdesc: Install and configure Oxygen XML Editor for productive DITA authoring with schema validation, visual editing, and built-in DITA-OT publishing.

p.b: After completing this chapter, you will be able to:

ul.li: Install and configure Oxygen XML Editor for DITA authoring
Use Author mode for WYSIWYG-style DITA editing
Validate DITA topics against the schema in real time
Publish directly from Oxygen using DITA-OT transformation scenarios
Set up Git for version-controlled DITA projects

context.p: Oxygen XML Editor is the industry-standard authoring tool for DITA. It provides schema-aware editing, WYSIWYG Author mode, a visual DITA map editor, and built-in DITA-OT integration — everything a technical writer needs in a single application.

ul.li: Oxygen XML Editor — Full-featured edition with Text, Author, and Grid views plus XSLT/XQuery debugging. Ideal for developers and content engineers.
Oxygen XML Author — Streamlined edition focused on Author (WYSIWYG) mode. Ideal for technical writers who prefer visual editing.

step.cmd: Download and install Oxygen XML Editor.

info.p: Go to oxygenxml.com and download the installer for your platform (Windows, macOS, or Linux). Run the installer and follow the prompts.
Oxygen includes a bundled JRE, so you do not need to install Java separately for the editor itself.
info.note: Oxygen offers a 30-day free trial. Academic and volume licenses are also available.

info.p: Open Options → Preferences → DITA and verify:

ul.li: DITA Open Toolkit: Point to your installed DITA-OT directory (e.g., ~/dita-ot-4.2). Oxygen bundles its own DITA-OT, but using your project's version ensures consistent output.
Default DITA-OT transtype: Set to html5 for quick preview builds.
Show DITA-OT console output: Enable this to see build logs directly in Oxygen.

step.cmd: Switch to Author mode for visual DITA editing.

info.p: Open any DITA topic and click the Author tab at the bottom of the editor (next to Text and Grid).
Author mode renders your DITA content visually — similar to a word processor — while maintaining the underlying XML structure. Key features:

ul.li: Content Completion Assistant: Press Enter inside an element to see valid child elements. Oxygen only offers elements allowed by the DITA DTD at the cursor position.
Inline validation: Invalid structure is underlined in red with a description of the problem.
Reuse actions: Right-click → Reuse to insert conrefs, keyrefs, or content key references.
Attribute editor: The Attributes panel (right side) shows all valid attributes for the selected element.

step.cmd: Create a new DITA topic using Oxygen templates.

info.p: Select File → New (or press Ctrl+N). In the New Document wizard:

ol.li: Choose a topic type: Concept, Task, or Reference
Enter the file name and click Create

info.p: Oxygen generates a valid skeleton with the correct DOCTYPE, root element, title, and body element — ready for you to fill in.

step.cmd: Open your DITA map in the DITA Maps Manager.

info.p: Open your .ditamap file. Oxygen displays it in the DITA Maps Manager panel (usually on the left), showing the topic hierarchy as a tree.
From the Maps Manager you can:

ul.li: Drag and drop topics to reorder them
Right-click → Insert Reference to add new topicrefs
Double-click any topic to open it for editing
Validate the entire map (check for broken references, missing topics)

step.cmd: Set up a transformation scenario for publishing.

info.p: With your ditamap open, click the Configure Transformation Scenario(s) button (wrench icon) or press Ctrl+Shift+T.

ol.li: Set Output directory: ${pd}/out/html5 (${pd} is the project directory)
Optionally add parameters: nav-toc=full for navigation
Click OK, then Apply Associated to run the build

info.p: The output opens automatically in your browser. You can re-run the same scenario with Ctrl+Shift+T → Apply Associated.
Oxygen validates DITA automatically as you type. You can also run manual validation:

ul.li: Single topic: Press Ctrl+Shift+V to validate the current file against its DTD
Entire map: In the DITA Maps Manager, click Validate and Check for Completeness. This checks all referenced topics for broken links, missing conrefs, and structural errors.

step.cmd: Initialize Git version control for your DITA project.

info.p: Oxygen includes a Git client plugin. Alternatively, use the command line:
info.codeblock:
cd myproject git init
# Create a .gitignore for DITA projects cat > .gitignore <<'EOF' out/ *.log .DS_Store temp/ EOF
git add . git commit -m "Initial DITA project structure"
info.p: In Oxygen, enable the Git client from Window → Show View → Git Staging to commit, push, and pull directly from the editor.

result.p: Your Oxygen XML authoring environment is configured. You have schema-aware DITA editing with Author mode, a visual map editor, transformation scenarios for publishing, validation, and Git version control.

ul.li: Visual Studio Code — Free, open-source editor with XML support via the Red Hat XML extension. Good for developers who prefer lightweight tooling. Requires manual snippet setup for DITA templates.
Any text editor — DITA is plain XML. Vim, Emacs, Sublime Text, and Notepad++ all work; you lose auto-complete, inline validation, and Author mode.
